meta.note: All cards are drawn from the lecture. Import into Anki/Quizlet as Q/A pairs.

cards.q: What three overlapping pieces of 'health' does the professor open with?
cards.a: Physical, mental, and spiritual health.

cards.q: How is 'spiritual health' defined in this lecture?
cards.a: Having a purpose in life — not necessarily being religious; linked to more positive outcomes.

cards.q: Which factor has the LARGEST impact on health (base of the pyramid)?
cards.a: Socioeconomic factors. Counseling/education has the smallest impact.

cards.q: In the professor's view, what is the biggest lever for improving health?
cards.a: Education — helping people understand why to follow a treatment plan and take their meds.

cards.q: Define pathogenic.
cards.a: Disease-causing — an organism's ability to cause disease.

cards.q: Define opportunistic pathogen.
cards.a: Normally harmless, but becomes pathogenic when the host is debilitated/immunocompromised or it lands in the wrong site.

cards.q: Define virulent.
cards.a: Having a high degree of ability to cause infection (e.g., Ebola, ~80% mortality).

cards.q: E. coli O157:H7 vs. gut E. coli — which is more virulent, and are both pathogenic?
cards.a: Both are pathogenic, but O157:H7 is more virulent (~20% get hemolytic uremic syndrome, ~20% mortality). Gut E. coli is opportunistic.

cards.q: What is hemolytic uremic syndrome?
cards.a: A serious E. coli O157:H7 complication — in the blood, attacking the kidneys, ~20% mortality.

cards.q: What is a portal of entry?
cards.a: The site where a microbe enters the body — cuts, lesions, injection sites, or natural orifices.

cards.q: Name the four portals of entry.
cards.a: Skin, mucous membranes, placenta, and the parenteral route.

cards.q: What is the parenteral route, and why does it matter clinically?
cards.a: Entry that bypasses skin/mucosa (e.g., IV drug use / shared needles) — spreads HIV and hepatitis B/C.

cards.q: Contamination vs. infection?
cards.a: Contamination = a microbe merely present on a surface/liquid. Infection = the organism evades defenses, multiplies, and becomes established in the body.

cards.q: Can an inanimate object be 'infected'?
cards.a: No. A cutting board can be contaminated (or a reservoir), but only a living host can be infected.

cards.q: How does a disease differ from an infection?
cards.a: A disease is a harmful alteration of tissue/metabolism and needs no microbe; an infection always involves a microbe growing in the body.

cards.q: Give examples of diseases that need no microbe.
cards.a: Cystic fibrosis, cancer, diabetes, CHD, depression, alcoholism, lupus (autoimmune).

cards.q: What does the suffix -itis tell you?
cards.a: Inflammation — usually a microbe interacting with the tissue (cellulitis, pharyngitis, cystitis, etc.).

cards.q: What is etiology?
cards.a: The study of the cause of a disease.

cards.q: Which five disease-classification terms did he say to know ('starting with immunological')?
cards.a: Immunological, neoplastic, iatrogenic, idiopathic, nosocomial.

cards.q: Define iatrogenic and idiopathic.
cards.a: Iatrogenic = caused by a medical procedure/treatment. Idiopathic = cause unknown.

cards.q: What are the modern replacements for 'nosocomial'?
cards.a: HAI (healthcare-associated infection) and CAI (community-acquired infection).

cards.q: Which disease-class terms are NOT tested?
cards.a: Heredity, congenital, degenerative, nutritional, endocrine, mental — 'outside the scope.'

cards.q: What are the three requirements of the infectious process?
cards.a: Enter host tissues, establish and grow, and obtain nutrients. Block any one and no infection develops.

cards.q: How does that connect to antibiotics?
cards.a: Antibiotics halt growth (step 2/3), which halts the infectious process. Example: HIV needs the T4 helper as its nutrient source.

cards.q: Intoxication vs. infection — timing and fever?
cards.a: Intoxication (toxin): short, sharp, NO fever. Infection (organism grows): longer, WITH fever.

cards.q: Compare S. aureus food poisoning vs. Salmonella foodborne infection.
cards.a: S. aureus intoxication: ~4 h onset, ~24 h, vomiting, no fever. Salmonella infection: 3–14 days, until treated, cramps/fever/diarrhea/dehydration/tenesmus.

cards.q: What is the giveaway of a bacterial infection?
cards.a: Fever. Intoxications never cause fever.

cards.q: What does S. aureus enterotoxin F cause?
cards.a: Toxic shock syndrome.

cards.q: Who was Typhoid Mary?
cards.a: Mary Mallon — a healthy carrier of Salmonella Typhi; a classic epidemiology story.

cards.q: Match the infectious agent to its cancer: HPV, H. pylori, Hepatitis B.
cards.a: HPV → cervical (also anal/oral); H. pylori → gastric; Hepatitis B → liver.

cards.q: Why do we screen (e.g., Pap smear) for these cancers?
cards.a: They take years to develop, so screening finds dysplastic/pre-cancer cells early enough to intervene.

cards.q: What is a virulence factor?
cards.a: A molecule a pathogen uses to colonize, evade immunity, enter/exit cells, and obtain nutrients from the host.

cards.q: Pathogenicity vs. virulence?
cards.a: Pathogenicity = the ability to cause disease; virulence = the degree of that pathogenicity.

cards.q: Name the four categories of virulence factors.
cards.a: Structural, toxins, enzymes, and anti-phagocytic factors.

cards.q: Give examples of STRUCTURAL virulence factors.
cards.a: Mycolic acid, peptidoglycan, capsule, viral envelope, endospore, flagella, fimbriae.

cards.q: What is adhesion, and why does a pathogen need it?
cards.a: Attaching to host cells (via adhesion factors, fimbriae, and attachment proteins) — required to establish colonies. On the slide, adhesion factors and biofilms fall under 'structural.'

cards.q: Exotoxin — source, chemistry, heat, fever?
cards.a: Actively secreted by living bacteria; protein; heat-labile (denaturable); causes NO fever; very toxic in tiny amounts.

cards.q: Endotoxin — what and where is it?
cards.a: The lipid A portion of the LPS in the Gram-negative cell wall.

cards.q: Endotoxin — toxicity, heat, fever?
cards.a: Low toxicity but needs high numbers; heat-stable; causes fever (it's a pyrogen), coagulation, and septic shock.

cards.q: Which toxin type can be made into a vaccine, and how?
cards.a: Exotoxins — denature them into a toxoid that keeps its epitopes, then make an antitoxin (IgG). Endotoxins can't be vaccinated against.

cards.q: Name the two most potent exotoxins and their organisms.
cards.a: Tetanospasmin (Clostridium tetani) and botulinum toxin (Clostridium botulinum).

cards.q: Why treat a heavy Gram-negative infection carefully?
cards.a: Bursting many cells at once releases more free lipid A (more surface area) → the patient can get worse.

cards.q: Three modes of action for exotoxins?
cards.a: Cytotoxin (kills cells), neurotoxin (affects nerves), enterotoxin (causes vomiting).

cards.q: What does lipase do as a virulence factor?
cards.a: Breaks down protective skin fatty acids (oleic acid) → pimples/blemishes (Staph, Strep).

cards.q: What does coagulase do, and who makes it?
cards.a: Coagulates plasma into a fibrin wall around bacteria → abscess/boil; made by Staphylococcus aureus.

cards.q: What is hyaluronidase, and why is it called the 'spread factor'?
cards.a: It destroys hyaluronic acid — the 'cement' holding cells together — so bacteria spread fast through tissue (S. pyogenes, S. aureus).

cards.q: Name the two anti-phagocytic proteins and their organisms.
cards.a: Protein A (Staphylococcus aureus) and M protein (Streptococcus pyogenes). Capsule counts too.

cards.q: Name the three lines of defense.
cards.a: 1st = physical/chemical barriers; 2nd = interferon, phagocytosis, complement; 3rd = adaptive immunity (B & T cells).

cards.q: What is innate immunity?
cards.a: Nonspecific defense that acts immediately or within hours of an antigen appearing.

cards.q: List several chemical (first-line) defenses.
cards.a: Sebum/oleic acid, lysozyme (tears/saliva), sweat lactic acid/electrolytes, acidic skin pH, stomach HCl, bile, semen, acidic vaginal pH.

cards.q: What are flushing mechanisms? Give examples.
cards.a: Physical washout of microbes: sneezing, coughing, lacrimation (tears), sweat, cerumen, salivation, defecation, peristalsis.

cards.q: What is the mucociliary escalator?
cards.a: Ciliated cells in the trachea/lungs sweep trapped debris and mucus up to the throat to be coughed out.

cards.q: Define diapedesis (and note the spelling).
cards.a: WBCs moving out of the circulatory system into the tissues to fight infection. Spelled with an I: d-i-a-p-e-d-e-s-i-s.

cards.q: What is complement?
cards.a: A cascade of at least 26 blood proteins that fire all at once ('like a sparkler').

cards.q: What four things does complement do?
cards.a: Neutralizes viruses, enhances phagocytosis, damages the plasma membrane, and lyses (destroys) the microorganism.

cards.q: Name the three complement pathways.
cards.a: Classical, lectin, and alternative (all reach a similar end result).

cards.q: What is interferon and how does it work?
cards.a: An antiviral/anti-cancer protein made by every cell in tiny amounts; it interferes with the transcription and translation of mRNA.

cards.q: Name the three types of interferon and a clinical use.
cards.a: Alpha, beta, and gamma. Betaseron (interferon-beta) is used for MS flares.

cards.q: How do normal flora protect us?
cards.a: Commensal/mutualistic organisms out-compete potential pathogens for space and nutrients.

cards.q: What is immunology the study of?
cards.a: The body's 2nd and 3rd lines of defense.

cards.q: Name the three jobs of a healthy immune system.
cards.a: Surveillance, recognition (of foreign material), and destruction of what's foreign.

cards.q: How do WBCs tell self from non-self, and what is non-self called?
cards.a: By reading markers on cell surfaces; non-self = antigen.

cards.q: Which cell is NOT marked as 'self,' and why does that matter?
cards.a: Red blood cells — they use a different marking system, which is why blood can be cross-typed and transfused.

cards.q: Why does a transplant need lifelong immunosuppressants?
cards.a: The donated organ carries the donor's markers, so the recipient's immune system sees it as non-self and would reject it.

cards.q: Which lymphocytes run humoral vs. cell-mediated immunity?
cards.a: B lymphocytes → humoral (antibodies); T lymphocytes → cell-mediated.
